refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999. Requires instrumentation of link health signals and timestamped logging.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The NGHE solution shall monitor and report link availability metrics in real-time.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The NGHE solution shall implement link redundancy or failover mechanisms to achieve ≥0.999 availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication path with high-reliability network infrastructure (fiber, dedicated bandwidth)
DP-B: Dual-path redundant communication (primary + secondary network, automatic failover)
DP-C: Multi-modal communication (cellular + satellite + mesh radio) with intelligent path selection
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary dual-path (cellular + private network) with satellite backup and local mesh fallback
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid multi-modal communication architecture with primary dual-path (cellular + private network), satellite backup, and local mesh fallback; include real-time link health monitoring, automatic failover logic, and availability reporting.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure selection (cellular provider, private network operator, satellite service), which is external to NGHE but must be contractually specified.
DP-D couples to vehicle-side communication hardware (modem, antenna, failover controller), which is a design dependency but contained within Vehicle module.
DP-D couples to tele-operator station communication hardware and software, which is a design dependency but contained within Teleoperation module.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are acceptable because they represent necessary dependencies for achieving the FR. The key is that DP-D does not create circular dependencies or hidden assumptions about uncontrolled external systems. Network infrastructure selection must be a contractual/operational decision, not a design assumption.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will specify and procure network infrastructure (cellular, private network, satellite) meeting minimum bandwidth and latency requirements.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and tele-operator stations will be equipped with multi-modal communication hardware (cellular modem, satellite transceiver, mesh radio).
RISK: Cellular network availability varies by geography; rural/remote sites may not achieve 0.999 with cellular alone.
RISK: Satellite latency (typically 500–700 ms) may be unacceptable for real-time teleoperation; satellite should be backup only.
RISK: Failover logic complexity increases software testing burden; incorrect failover can degrade availability.
RISK: Cost of multi-modal hardware and service subscriptions may exceed customer budget.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly state: (1) Primary path: dual-mode (cellular + private network) with sub-100ms latency requirement; (2) Secondary path: satellite with >500ms latency acceptable for non-real-time telemetry; (3) Tertiary path: local mesh radio for site-local fallback; (4) Failover logic: automatic switchover on primary path loss, with operator override capability; (5) Monitoring: continuous link health telemetry with 1-second reporting interval.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the tele-operator station hardware and software, including multi-modal communication hardware (cellular modem, satellite transceiver, mesh radio), link health monitoring logic, failover decision logic, and operator interface for link status and manual failover control. The module is responsible for initiating and maintaining the uplink (command transmission) and receiving/processing the downlink (telemetry reception).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) to Vehicle via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle state, sensor data, and operational telemetry from Vehicle via Telemetry interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives safety-critical alerts (link loss, emergency stop, hazard detection) from Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with external network infrastructure (cellular, private network, satellite, mesh) via Network Link interface for bidirectional communication.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of tele-operator station hardware, communication hardware, link monitoring, and failover logic. High containment because all uplink/downlink logic and operator-facing link management resides here. Minor diffusion: link health metrics may be reported to Safety Services and Vehicle for coordinated response.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side communication hardware and receives commands/sends telemetry, but does not own the tele-operator station or link monitoring logic. Moderate containment because vehicle-side failover (e.g., switching to mesh radio) may be needed, but primary failover logic should reside in Teleoperation. Diffusion: Vehicle must implement redundant communication paths and respond to link loss events.

Safety Services owns safety-critical response to link loss (e.g., emergency stop, safe state transition), but does not own the communication infrastructure or link monitoring. Moderate-low containment because link availability is a safety concern but not the primary safety function. Diffusion: Safety Services must monitor link health and trigger safety actions on link loss.

NGHE Solution is the system-level owner of the overall availability requirement, but the actual implementation is distributed across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services. Low containment because this is a system-level concern requiring coordination across multiple modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the primary owner of teleoperation link availability because it controls the tele-operator station hardware, communication hardware, link monitoring logic, and failover decision logic. The module interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and external network infrastructure (via Network Link). This assignment minimizes diffusion and creates a clear ownership boundary for link availability.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle-side communication hardware fails or is misconfigured, Teleoperation module cannot compensate; requires clear interface contract and Vehicle module testing.
RISK: If Safety Services does not respond correctly to SafetyAlert (link loss), the system may not achieve safe state; requires coordinated testing and safety validation.

RISK: If external network infrastructure (cellular, satellite) is unavailable, Teleoperation module cannot achieve 0.999 availability; requires contractual SLA with network providers and customer acceptance of geographic limitations.
RISK: Failover logic complexity in Teleoperation module may introduce bugs; requires rigorous testing and formal verification of failover state machine.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish clear interface contracts: (1) Teleoperation <-> Vehicle: Tasking (command format, latency, retry logic) and Telemetry (data format, heartbeat interval); (2) Teleoperation <-> Safety Services: SafetyAlert (link loss detection, emergency stop trigger); (3) Teleoperation <-> Network Link: link health metrics (latency, packet loss, path status). Define acceptance criteria for each interface to ensure coordinated availability achievement.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid multi-modal communication with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach (dual-path primary, satellite backup, mesh fallback) that is technically feasible and aligns with high safety criticality. The DP is not over-constrained and allows flexibility in network infrastructure selection.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic is primarily in Teleoperation module, but Vehicle module must implement corresponding failover on the vehicle-side communication hardware; requires tight interface contract and coordinated testing.
Link health monitoring is in Teleoperation module, but Safety Services must also monitor link status to trigger safety actions; requires clear signal flow and event notification mechanism.
Network infrastructure (cellular, satellite, mesh) is external to NGHE modules; requires contractual SLA and customer acceptance of geographic/infrastructure dependencies.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) for link availability logic. Diffusion is acceptable because it is limited to well-defined interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) and does not create hidden dependencies or circular couplings. Vehicle and Safety Services modules have clear responsibilities for their respective failover and safety actions.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via continuous monitoring of link state during evaluation period. Requires instrumentation of link health signals (latency, packet loss, path status) and timestamped logging. Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999. Testing should include failure injection (simulate cellular loss, satellite latency, mesh radio interference) to validate failover logic.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns tele-operator station hardware, communication hardware, link monitoring, and failover logic. Vehicle module owns vehicle-side communication hardware and failover response. Safety Services owns safety-critical response to link loss. NGHE Solution owns system-level availability requirement and acceptance criteria.

recommended_revisions: Establish detailed interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link to ensure coordinated failover and link monitoring.
Define link health metrics (latency, packet loss, path status) and reporting intervals (e.g., 1-second) to enable real-time monitoring and failover decision-making.

Specify failover logic state machine (primary path active, primary path loss detected, secondary path activation, secondary path loss detected, tertiary path activation) with timing and retry parameters.

Define acceptance criteria for each network path (cellular: <100ms latency, >99.5% availability; satellite: <700ms latency, >99.0% availability; mesh: <500ms latency, >95% availability) to ensure composite availability ≥0.999.
Establish safety validation plan for link loss scenarios (e.g., loss of cellular, loss of satellite, loss of mesh) to ensure Safety Services responds correctly.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (uplink, downlink, monitoring, redundancy). DP-D is sound and well-justified, but requires further refinement at the next decomposition level to address sub-FRs and define detailed failover logic, link health monitoring, and interface contracts. Teleoperation module assignment is appropriate and achieves high containment. Proceeding to next level will clarify failover state machine, link health metrics, and interface specifications.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with <100ms latency and >99.5% availability.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with <100ms latency and >99.5% availability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The NGHE solution shall monitor and report link availability metrics (latency, packet loss, path status) in real-time with 1-second reporting interval.

FR-NGHE-010.4: The NGHE solution shall implement automatic failover from primary path (cellular + private network) to secondary path (satellite) to tertiary path (mesh radio) on link loss detection, with operator override capability.

assumptions: Customer will specify and procure network infrastructure (cellular, private network, satellite) meeting minimum bandwidth and latency requirements.
Vehicle and tele-operator stations will be equipped with multi-modal communication hardware (cellular modem, satellite transceiver, mesh radio).
Failover logic can be implemented in software with acceptable latency (<1 second for path switchover).
Link health metrics (latency, packet loss) can be measured and reported with 1-second granularity.
Safety Services will respond to link loss alerts within acceptable time window (e.g., <5 seconds for emergency stop).
open_risks: Cellular network availability varies by geography; rural/remote sites may not achieve 0.999 with cellular alone.
Satellite latency (500–700 ms) may be unacceptable for real-time teleoperation; requires validation that satellite is suitable for backup only.
Failover logic complexity increases software testing burden; incorrect failover can degrade availability.
Cost of multi-modal hardware and service subscriptions may exceed customer budget.
Interference between multiple communication paths (cellular, satellite, mesh) may degrade performance; requires RF analysis and testing.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure availability data (cellular, private network, satellite) for target geographic regions.
Communication hardware specifications (latency, packet loss, failover time) for cellular modem, satellite transceiver, mesh radio.
Failover logic design and state machine specification with timing parameters.
Link health monitoring algorithm and metrics definition (latency, packet loss, path status).
Safety validation plan for link loss scenarios and Safety Services response testing.
Cost analysis for multi-modal hardware and service subscriptions.
RF analysis and interference testing for multi-modal communication paths.
